systems.name: Melody Harmonization Hierarchy System
systems.summary: The book's central system is a progressive hierarchy of harmonization devices applied to a single-note melody sourced from a lead sheet. The members are the harmonization intervals — unison/octave (bare melody), thirds below, tenths (octave-displaced thirds functioning as bass), and sixths below — each successive member adding harmonic density while following shared chord-tone matching logic. The system governs how a player traverses from one harmonization layer to the next (adding or swapping intervals) and how each member is modified (adjusting non-chord tones, varying rhythm, handling altered extensions). The unifying rule across all members is that melody primacy must be preserved and that the underlying chord symbol is the arbiter of acceptability, with the ear as the final court of appeal.

members.name: Bare Single-Line Melody
members.summary: The unharmonized melody derived from a lead sheet; the entry-point member of the hierarchy.

members.name: Thirds Below (Parallel Thirds Harmonization)
members.summary: A harmony note a diatonic third below each melody note, played with free stroke on adjacent strings.

members.name: Tenths (Octave-Displaced Third as Bass)
members.summary: The third dropped an octave lower so it functions as an independent bass note beneath the melody.

members.name: Sixths Below (Diatonic Sixth Harmonization)
members.summary: A harmony note a diatonic sixth below the melody, functioning as a melody shadow or bass note, with the key note kept on top.

members.name: Root/Fifth Oom-Pah Bass
members.summary: Alternating root and fifth of each chord in the bass as the foundational accompaniment underneath the melody.

traversal_rules.name: Add Bass to Complete Three-Part Texture
traversal_rules.summary: When thirds harmonization is present, add root/fifth bass to complete the melody-harmony-bass three-voice arrangement.

references.quote_excerpt: Put the bass back in. Even a simple root/root and root/fifth bass can turn the melody with thirds into a full three-part arrangement

traversal_rules.name: Promote Thirds to Tenths with Independent Bass Rhythm
traversal_rules.summary: When moving from parallel thirds to tenths, assign the tenth its own independent rhythmic pattern rather than doubling the melody rhythm.

references.topic: Parallel tenths vs independent bass rhythm
references.quote_excerpt: the tenth does often function as a bass note, but only infrequently does it move parallel to the melodic rhythm. More often it has its own independent rhythm

traversal_rules.name: Rhythmic Counterpoint Between Melody Density and Bass Density
traversal_rules.summary: When the melody is rhythmically busy, use longer bass note values; when the melody is sparse, use a busier bass part.

references.topic: Bass rhythm vs melody density rule
references.quote_excerpt: If the rhythm of the melody is busy and many-noted, you can complement it by playing longer notes in the bass
Changing the bass accompaniment rhythm can change the mood or character of a piece

traversal_rules.name: Abandon Arpeggiation When Melody Leaps Unpredictably
traversal_rules.summary: Switch away from arpeggiation texture to another accompaniment technique when the melody continually leaps wildly.

references.quote_excerpt: If you notice that the melody of the tune you wish to arrange continually leaps up and down wildly, abandon arpeggiation for that particular tune

traversal_rules.name: Keep Key Note on Top When Traversing to Sixths
traversal_rules.summary: When applying sixth-below harmonization, the key note must remain on top to preserve major tonality.

references.topic: Table of Sixths key note on top rule
references.quote_excerpt: scales harmonized in sixths have the key note of each scale on top, with the harmony note a sixth below. If the key note were in the bottom position the ear would perceive the scale as being in a minor key

modification_rules.name: Omit Bass Third When Melody Already Supplies It
modification_rules.summary: When the melody note is itself a major or minor third of the chord, do not add a redundant third in the bass; use root or fifth instead.

references.quote_excerpt: when the melody is a major or minor third, the bass note is either a root or fifth, and when the melody is other than a third, the third is used in the bass to harmonically identify the chord
If the melody in that measure already contains the major or minor third of the major or minor chord indicated above, no additional third is required

modification_rules.name: Accept Non-Chord-Tone Thirds That Pass the Ear Test
modification_rules.summary: When a third-below harmonization lands on a non-chord tone, accept it if it passes an ear test; adjust to a nearby chord tone only if needed.

references.topic: non-chord tone thirds adjust or not
references.quote_excerpt: Non-chord tones make for the interesting spice in an otherwise dull chord-tone-only dish. It's your ear and your decision

modification_rules.name: Adjust Sixths That Imply Unwanted Harmonic Extensions
modification_rules.summary: When a diatonic sixth implies an extension (seventh, ninth) not intended by the chord symbol, optionally adjust the sixth to a strict chord tone.

references.topic: Melody on fifth sixth below is minor seventh
references.quote_excerpt: The minor seventh interval is contained in the C scale, so it is not a clash. You can change the C to D if you wish to keep the sound to a simple Dm chord

modification_rules.name: Sharpen or Flatten Fifth for Altered Chord Symbols
modification_rules.summary: When the chord symbol specifies an altered fifth (#5 or b5), raise or lower the bass fifth accordingly rather than deriving it from the plain major scale.

references.quote_excerpt: If the chord is c7#s, raise the fifth a half step, and if it is C7>5, lower the fifth a half step

modification_rules.name: Use Mixed-Rhythm Bass to De-Emphasize Strict Oom-Pah
modification_rules.summary: Replace strict alternating root/fifth with quarter, half, or whole notes in mixed combinations for rhythmic variety.

references.quote_excerpt: Another way of de-emphasizing the oom-pah accompaniment while still using only root/fifth is to have bass notes with mixed rhythm moving in quarter, half or whole notes

modification_rules.name: Prioritize Musical Effect Over Sustain Perfection
modification_rules.summary: Accept shorter note durations in arpeggiated harmonization when fingering constraints prevent full sustain; overall effect supersedes mechanical completeness.

references.quote_excerpt: Go for the overall effect, and don't get crazy over the shorter duration of a single note

modification_rules.name: More-Is-Less Arranging Restraint
modification_rules.summary: Prefer slow-moving bass, call-and-answer sequences, and selective harmonic devices over dense layering of all available techniques simultaneously.

references.quote_excerpt: here is a tune that isn't cluttered with every arranging device, but is still totally whole, missing nothing
In all of the tunes here I like to imply more with less. Chords, for instance, are most often only two or three notes, and the melody is all-important

systems.name: Jazz Harmonic Movement System
systems.summary: This system governs how harmonic motion is constructed and navigated in fingerstyle jazz arrangements. Its members are the three primary harmonic movement types the book identifies: diatonic (scale-step), cycle-of-fifths (descending perfect fifths), and chromatic (half-step root movement), plus the structural deployment contexts (intro, ending, turnaround, tag, modulation). Traversal rules describe how to move between chords using these types — particularly voice-leading by half or whole step rather than parallel root motion. Modification rules govern how individual chord members are altered at structural moments: intro gateway requirements, tag substitution, modulation methods, and the substitution of II–V7 for direct dominant motion. The system treats harmonic movement as several simultaneously moving voices rather than block-chord substitution.

members.name: Diatonic Harmony (Scale-Step Movement)
members.summary: Harmonic movement by whole and half steps within a single key.

members.name: Cycle of Fifths (Descending Perfect Fifth Movement)
members.summary: Harmonic movement descending by perfect fifths (or ascending fourths), the most basic harmonic movement of jazz.

members.name: Chromatic Root Movement (Half-Step Progression)
members.summary: Harmonic movement in which chord roots move by half steps, with diminished seventh as the standard connector chord for ascending sequences.

members.name: Structural Deployment Contexts
members.summary: Formal positions within a performance — intro, ending, turnaround, tag, and modulation — each with specific harmonic requirements.

members.name: Seventh Chord (Minimum Jazz Harmonic Unit)
members.summary: Four-note chords built by stacking thirds (1-3-5-7); the minimum harmonic unit of jazz harmony.

traversal_rules.name: Voice-Lead by Step Rather Than Root-Position Block Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Prefer moving individual voices by half-step, whole-step, or mixed intervals over always placing the root in the bass; think of harmonic movement as several moving voices.

references.quote_excerpt: Always playing chords with the root in the bass works, but may not be as smooth or interesting as moving by half-step or whole-step intervals or mixed movement
If you think of harmonic movement as several moving voices rather than a glob of dots on a chord box grid, your arrangements and improvisations will be more airy, more interesting and less clunky

traversal_rules.name: Contrary Motion for V7–I Resolution
traversal_rules.summary: In V7–I progressions, accompany a descending top voice with an opposing (contrary) bass movement rather than parallel motion.

references.quote_excerpt: As the top note descends by half step the root note has its own opposing pattern, not parallel to the top

traversal_rules.name: Tritone Pull Drives V7–I Resolution
traversal_rules.summary: The tritone interval within a dominant seventh chord (b7 and 3) creates tension that resolves by half step to the tonic chord tones.

references.quote_excerpt: you can just feel the tug of the unresolved B' and E (the tritone interval of a-5) being pulled by the A and F in the resolved F chord

traversal_rules.name: Spread Texture: Bass Alternates Up a Fourth / Down a Fifth
traversal_rules.summary: Create a spread texture by alternating bass notes up a fourth and then down a fifth, producing a tenth interval from the melody above.

references.quote_excerpt: The root bass notes alternate in moving up a fourth and then down a fifth. Each new bass note is a tenth interval from the melody note above

traversal_rules.name: Use Chromatic Bass-Note Choice to Maintain Chromatic Continuity
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chords of different qualities, select the bass voicing note (root, third, fifth) that continues a chromatic line rather than jumping to the root.

references.quote_excerpt: the lowest note of Gmaj7 in the first measure is D, the fifth, and the lowest note of G7(5) is b5. This was done to continue the effect of chromatic movement from Gmaj7 to G7(5) to Cmaj7

traversal_rules.name: Use Diminished Seventh as Connector for Ascending Chromatic Movement
traversal_rules.summary: When root movement ascends by half steps, insert a diminished seventh chord as the in-between connector chord.

references.quote_excerpt: In many cases the diminished seventh chord is the in-between, the hamburger helper of ascending harmonic movement

modification_rules.name: Intro Gateway: Final Chord Must Be V7 or Substitute
modification_rules.summary: Any intro must close on a V7 or V7 substitute chord to create the harmonic gateway into the first chord of the tune.

references.quote_excerpt: The last chord of the intro must be a V7 or V7 substitute in order to move into the first chord of the tune itself

modification_rules.name: Tag Initiation: Substitute V7 for Final I Chord
modification_rules.summary: To initiate a tag/coda, replace the final I chord with a V7 (or substitute) chord, extending the harmonic motion.

references.quote_excerpt: the replacement of the usual final I chord—E, here—with a V7 (or other substitute) chord is required

modification_rules.name: II–V7–I as Most Idiomatic Modulation Bridge
modification_rules.summary: When modulating between keys, use a II–V7–I of the new key as the smoothest and most idiomatic jazz bridge; this is preferred over bare V7 substitution or back-cycling alone.

references.quote_excerpt: the most common jazz progression unit is II-V7-I. Use it here then, by putting a II of the upcoming new key in front of the new key V-7

modification_rules.name: Reuse Song's Final Measures as Instant Intro
modification_rules.summary: The last four or eight measures of the tune itself, including its melody, can directly serve as an intro, reducing preparation overhead.

references.quote_excerpt: the last four- or eight measures of the tune itself can often be used, including the melody

modification_rules.name: Drop Root/Fifth for Rootless Tritone/Fourth Voicing in Ensemble
modification_rules.summary: In ensemble settings where the bassist supplies root and fifth, voice chords as alternating pairs of fourths and tritones (major/minor seventh to major third) without explicitly stating the root.

references.quote_excerpt: There is no explicitly stated root or fifth of the chord. If you play in a group, the bassist will cover roots and fifths

modification_rules.name: Two Additional Notes Suffice for a Full-Sounding Arrangement
modification_rules.summary: A melody accompanied by only two inner-voice notes produces a full arrangement; do not add density beyond what the ear requires.

references.quote_excerpt: A melody accompanied by two additional notes is often all you need to make an arrangement sound full, whether you are playing by yourself or with a group

modification_rules.name: Deceptive Cadence bVI Substitution for Modulation
modification_rules.summary: Insert a bVI chord after diatonic cycle movement to open alternative harmonic paths as a modulation device.

references.quote_excerpt: a VI (A-6) of the C scale is inserted. This deceptive cadence opens up several possibilities

systems.name: Self-Accompaniment Texture System
systems.summary: This system organizes the toolkit of self-accompaniment textures available to a solo fingerstyle jazz guitarist. Its members are the distinct bass-and-chord textures arrayed from simplest to most complex: root-movement bass, doubled bass, root/fifth alternation, walking bass with chromatic approach notes, arpeggiation, two-note inner voices, moving tenths, and bass-first walking-with-chord. Traversal rules govern when and how to move from one texture to another — primarily based on ensemble context (solo vs. group), melodic density, and stylistic period. Modification rules govern how individual textures are altered: applying anticipation/delay to any bass line, adjusting voicing register for ensemble, and choosing bass-first vs. chord-first conception. The system's governing aesthetic is that musical effect and coherence outrank mechanical interval adherence.

members.name: Root-Movement Bass
members.summary: Simplest accompaniment: bass notes match chord root names directly.

members.name: Doubled Bass Notes
members.summary: Two same bass notes per measure; swing-era upright bass style.

members.name: Root/Fifth Alternation
members.summary: Bass alternates between root and fifth of each chord; folk, country, and jazz oom-pah idiom.

members.name: Walking Bass with Chromatic Approach Notes
members.summary: Jazz walking bass line using chromatic approach notes to target each chord.

members.name: Arpeggiation
members.summary: Broken-chord texture appropriate for slower tunes; p plays lower strings, i/m play higher strings with free stroke.

members.name: Two-Note Inner-Voice Voicing
members.summary: Sparse two-note accompaniment producing full texture with minimal voice count.

members.name: Moving Tenths (Stride Piano Device)
members.summary: Sustained half-note or quarter-note tenths in the bass, adapted from swing-era piano practice.

members.name: Bass-First Walking-Bass-Plus-Chord
members.summary: Offset bass note preceding the chord by a quarter or eighth note; conceived bass-first for maximum harmonic flexibility.

traversal_rules.name: Conceive Bass Line Before Chord in Walking-Bass Texture
traversal_rules.summary: When building a walking-bass-plus-chord texture, plan or improvise the bass line first and add chords afterward; committing to chord fingering first severely restricts bass choices.

references.quote_excerpt: Think bass-plus-chord, not chord-plus-bass. It's best to conceive of a bass line first—planned or improvised—and then maybe add a chord

traversal_rules.name: Select Arpeggiation Texture for Slower-Moving Tunes
traversal_rules.summary: Choose arpeggiation as the accompaniment texture when the tune moves slowly; project the melody note clearly within the arpeggio.

references.quote_excerpt: For slower moving tunes, arpeggiation is a natural for fingerstyle playing. Don't forget to emphasize the melody note so that it won't get lost

traversal_rules.name: Voice High on Top Four/Five Strings When Electric Bass Present
traversal_rules.summary: In ensemble with electric bass, move all chord voicings to the upper four or five strings to avoid muddiness in the low register.

references.quote_excerpt: play your rhythm part, whether constant or comping, higher on the board on the top four or at most five strings

traversal_rules.name: Comping Density: More in Holes, Less When Soloist Is Active
traversal_rules.summary: When accompanying a soloist, increase harmonic/rhythmic density during the soloist's rests and reduce it when the soloist plays busily.

references.quote_excerpt: play more when the soloist is not playing, and play less when he is. Play more when there are 'holes' to be filled

modification_rules.name: Apply Anticipation or Delay to Bass Line for Rhythmic Variety
modification_rules.summary: Lead the bass note ahead of the beat (anticipation) or delay it behind the beat to avoid four-on-the-floor heaviness.

references.quote_excerpt: Leading with a bass note is a way to give variety to accompaniment instead of just chugging along with four bass notes to a measure
The opposite of leading with the lower line is trailing or delaying it. This too steers away from heaviness

modification_rules.name: Prioritize Effect Over Strict Interval Pattern
modification_rules.summary: When varying alternating bass patterns, maintaining the low-to-high alternation effect matters more than preserving the exact interval (root/fifth, root/seventh, etc.).

references.quote_excerpt: Keeping a strict pattern of root/seventh is not as important as long as the effect (low to high alternation) is there

modification_rules.name: Reframe Melody/Bass Overlap as Valid Arrangement Solution
modification_rules.summary: When the melody note merges with the top voice of a two-note bass accompaniment, treat this as a valid arrangement solution rather than a voice-leading problem to fix.

references.quote_excerpt: Often the melody may merge with and become the top part of the two note bass accompaniment. It's not a problem, it's a solution

modification_rules.name: Avoid Constant Bass Note Doubling in Modern Style
modification_rules.summary: Unless recreating vintage jazz, do not double bass notes constantly (two same notes per measure); modern style favors more variety.

references.quote_excerpt: Today the bass doesn't often play four quarter notes to the bar, especially during the first and last chorus. Unless you, as the guitarist/bassist, want to recreate vintage jazz, avoid constant doubling
